notes: Ikhwan al-Safa corpus used as the worked example domain for schema design
Neoplatonism is the scholarly domain modeled in the schema exercise
The Brethren of Purity analytical table used as a worked example of structured LLM output
Neoplatonic texts as the test domain for structured extraction prompts
Ficino as a key node in the Neoplatonism transmission graph
Plotinus as the origin node in the Neoplatonism transmission
The transmission of Neoplatonism is the worked graph example in this lesson
Emanation as the key conceptual edge type in the intellectual history graph
Pico was Ficino’s student and collaborator at the Platonic Academy in Florence
Ficino’s entire philosophical project was a recovery and Christianization of Plotinian Neoplatonism
The Rasail presents an emanationist cosmology central to the Brethren’s metaphysics
Plotinus is the originator of the systematic emanation doctrine in the Enneads
The Rasail is the primary text authored by the Brethren of Purity
The Oration on the Dignity of Man is Pico’s most famous text
Ficino’s project was the recovery of Platonic philosophy; he produced the first complete Latin translation of Plato
Ficino translated the Corpus Hermeticum before completing his Plato translations, treating Hermes as the founding figure of the prisca theologia
Ficino theorised anima mundi extensively in De vita coelitus comparanda, arguing the world has a living soul mediating between intellect and matter
Ficino developed a sophisticated pneumatology in which spiritus is the subtle vehicle through which stellar influences are transmitted to the human body and soul
De vita III is the primary Renaissance text of theoretical and practical astral magic, explaining how to draw down planetary spiritus
Ficino coined and developed the doctrine of prisca theologia, the ancient theology underlying all genuine wisdom traditions from Hermes and Zoroaster to Plato
Ficino produced the first Latin translation of the Corpus Hermeticum in 1463; it shaped his philosophical project in fundamental ways
Pico della Mirandola was the first Latin thinker to systematically incorporate Jewish Kabbalah into Christian humanist philosophy, pioneering Christian Kabbalah
Pico expanded the prisca theologia into a concordia philosophorum, claiming all philosophical traditions ultimately agree on fundamental truths
De occulta philosophia directly builds on Ficino’s theories of spiritus, astral magic, and world soul as foundational premises
Agrippa knew and incorporated Pico’s Kabbalistic work, especially the nine hundred theses, into his own three-book synthesis
Book III of De occulta philosophia is devoted to Christian Kabbalah, treating divine names, sephirot, and angelic hierarchies as the highest magical level
Book II of De occulta philosophia is a comprehensive treatment of celestial magic, including planetary seals, characters, and astral correspondences
Agrippa systematised the planetary kameas (magic squares) in De occulta philosophia Book II, providing the definitive Renaissance treatment of their magical use
De occulta philosophia is Agrippa’s primary and defining work, the great synthesis of Renaissance occult philosophy
Bruno extends Ficinian astral magic and memory theory into a more radical and explicitly magical direction, treating the cosmos as a living animated system
Bruno transformed the classical art of memory into a magical system charged with astrological images, aiming at a universal magical memory linking the operator to cosmic forces
Bruno’s natural magic and cosmology are explicitly Hermetic; he regarded Hermes Trismegistus as the highest ancient authority and his own philosophy as a Hermetic reform
Paracelsus transformed alchemy from a metallurgical art into iatrochemistry, applying alchemical principles to medicine and founding a new chemical pharmacopoeia
Paracelsus explicitly and polemically rejected the Aristotelian-Galenic medical tradition, publicly burning Avicenna’s Canon and claiming experience over ancient authority
Dee owned and annotated De occulta philosophia and knew Agrippa’s work well; it informed his own angelic and Kabbalistic experiments
Dee’s Enochian angelic system has significant structural parallels to Kabbalistic frameworks of divine names and angelic hierarchies
De occulta philosophia was one of Dee’s key sources; his library copy survives with his annotations
Fludd’s entire cosmological system in Utriusque cosmi historia is Hermetic in foundation, presenting the macrocosm-microcosm correspondence as Hermetic truth
Fludd incorporates Kabbalistic sephirot and divine name hierarchies into his elaborate cosmological diagrams, blending them with Christian and Hermetic frameworks
Fludd builds on Agrippa’s three-world synthesis and encyclopedic approach to occult philosophy, developing it into an even more elaborately illustrated cosmological system
Kircher’s Oedipus Aegyptiacus decodes Egyptian hieroglyphs through the lens of Hermetic philosophy, treating Egypt as the source of prisca theologia
Kircher applied Kabbalistic methods of letter mysticism and sephirotic analysis to his interpretation of Egyptian hieroglyphs and ancient religious symbols
Kircher situated himself within the Hermetic-Neoplatonic lineage descending from Ficino, treating the prisca theologia as the organising framework of universal history
Proclus provided the most rigorous and systematic elaboration of Plotinian Neoplatonism, formalising its logic in the Elements of Theology
Proclus inherited and continued the theurgic synthesis of Iamblichus, integrating ritual practice into the metaphysical system of late Neoplatonism
Proclus provided the most philosophically sophisticated account of theurgy, explaining how ritual acts ascend through the emanative hierarchy to reach the divine
Proclus gave the most detailed and formally rigorous elaboration of emanation doctrine, distinguishing multiple levels within each hypostasis with great precision
Iamblichus was a pupil of Porphyry, himself Plotinus’s direct student; he transformed Plotinian metaphysics by insisting on the necessity of theurgic ritual for ascent
De mysteriis is the primary theoretical and apologetic text of theurgy, defending ritual practice against philosophical objections and systematising its metaphysical basis
Iamblichus modified Plotinus’s emanationism by introducing additional levels of hypostasis and insisting more strongly on the radical transcendence of the One
Porphyry was Plotinus’s direct student and literary executor, editing and publishing the Enneads and writing the primary biography of Plotinus
Porphyry critiqued the legitimacy of theurgic ritual in his Letter to Anebo, generating Iamblichus’s rebuttal in De mysteriis and defining the central debate of late Neoplatonism
The Pseudo-Dionysian corpus Christianises Proclus almost wholesale, transposing his emanative hierarchy and terminology into a Christian theological framework
Pseudo-Dionysius was the primary conduit through which Neoplatonic thought entered mainstream Christian theology and mysticism in the medieval West
Pseudo-Dionysius reframes Neoplatonic emanation as divine procession (proodos), the outward movement of divine goodness creating and sustaining all beings
Ibn Sina built systematically on Al-Farabi’s synthesis of Aristotelian and Neoplatonic philosophy, extending it into medicine, psychology, and metaphysics
Ibn Sina’s philosophy operates within an Aristotelian framework across logic, natural philosophy, psychology, and metaphysics, substantially modified by Neoplatonic elements
Ibn Sina adapted Neoplatonic emanationism for an Islamic philosophical context, developing a ten-intellect cosmology in which the Active Intellect emanates the sublunar world
Ibn Rushd (Averroes) was the definitive Aristotelian commentator whose interpretations shaped both Islamic and Latin scholastic philosophy for centuries
Ibn Rushd extensively critiqued Ibn Sina’s Neoplatonising interpretation of Aristotle, arguing it distorted genuine Aristotelianism with Platonic accretions
Al-Farabi founded the Islamic Aristotelian tradition, producing systematic commentaries and summaries of Aristotle’s logic, natural philosophy, and political philosophy
Al-Farabi sought to synthesise Plato and Aristotle, arguing their philosophies were ultimately in agreement, and drew on Platonic political philosophy for his ideal state
Al-Farabi developed a ten-intellect emanationist cosmology in which the First Cause overflows into successive intellects down to the Active Intellect governing the sublunar realm
Al-Buni systematised planetary magic squares and letter-number correspondences, providing the most detailed Arabic treatment of their construction and use in Islamic occult tradition
Al-Buni’s letter mysticism and methods of divine name manipulation run in close parallel to Kabbalistic approaches, raising questions of shared sources or mutual influence
Shams al-Maarif is Al-Buni’s primary work and the most influential compendium of Islamic occult science
Abraham Abulafia founded the ecstatic or prophetic strand of Kabbalah, centred on letter permutation, divine name meditation, and techniques for achieving prophetic consciousness
Abulafia’s entire system of letter permutation mysticism derives from and elaborates the letter-combination methods of Sefer Yetzirah, treated as the key to prophetic technique
Albertus Magnus was the greatest medieval Aristotelian encyclopedist, producing comprehensive paraphrases of the entire Aristotelian corpus and naturalising it within Christian learning
Albertus catalogued and evaluated alchemical literature, including in his De mineralibus, and was credited (probably falsely) with important alchemical texts in the later tradition
Albertus drew heavily on Avicenna’s natural philosophical writings, particularly on mineralogy, psychology, and cosmology, integrating them into his Aristotelian synthesis
Plotinus presented his philosophy as a systematic development of Plato, treating the Enneads as the recovery of Plato’s true and hidden metaphysical teaching
Plotinus is the founder of Neoplatonism as a distinct philosophical tradition, establishing its characteristic doctrines of the One, Intellect, and Soul in the Enneads
Plotinus elaborated the World Soul as the third great hypostasis in the emanative hierarchy, mediating between the Intellect and the material world
Roger Bacon worked within the Aristotelian natural philosophical tradition while pushing its empirical dimensions toward experimental method and mathematical optics
Bacon drew on Avicenna’s optics and natural philosophy, especially his theories of species and the multiplication of forms, as key sources for his own scientific programme
Bacon endorsed alchemy as a legitimate branch of natural philosophy capable of producing artificial gold and extending human life, situating it within his broader scientific vision
Neoplatonism absorbed and substantially amplified the Pythagorean heritage, treating number mysticism and harmonic cosmology as an anticipation of Platonic metaphysics
Hermes Trismegistus is the credited author and divine speaker of the Corpus Hermeticum dialogues; his authority as sage legitimated the texts for Renaissance readers
Hermes Trismegistus is the founding figure of the Hermetic tradition; all Hermetic philosophy is conducted under his authority as the thrice-great sage of Egypt
The Picatrix is the most comprehensive manual of astral magic in the Western tradition, systematically presenting talismanic theory, planetary ritual, and astrological image magic
The Picatrix explains the pneumatic mechanism by which astral influence descends through spiritus to affect material substances and prepared talismans
De mysteriis Aegyptiorum is the primary theoretical text of theurgy, providing the philosophical defence and systematic account of ritual ascent in late Neoplatonism
De mysteriis is attributed to Iamblichus and is his most influential work; it defines his contribution to theurgic Neoplatonism
The Enneads is the collected philosophical writings of Plotinus, edited by Porphyry; it is the foundational text of the Neoplatonic tradition
The Enneads contains the original and most authoritative exposition of the emanation of the One, Intellect, and Soul as the three primary hypostases
The Corpus Hermeticum is the primary textual foundation of Hermeticism, presenting dialogues on cosmology, gnosis, and the soul’s ascent attributed to Hermes Trismegistus
The Zohar is the central and most authoritative text of the Kabbalistic tradition, presenting mystical commentary on Torah through the lens of the ten sefirot
Sefer Yetzirah is the earliest surviving Kabbalistic text, presenting the creation of the world through the thirty-two paths of wisdom: ten sefirot and twenty-two Hebrew letters
The World Soul is the third emanation in the Plotinian hierarchy, proceeding from the Intellect, which itself proceeds from the One; it is structurally inseparable from emanation theory
Theurgy presupposes the emanative hierarchy as its metaphysical framework; ritual acts work precisely by engaging the sympathetic links within the descending chain of emanations
Spiritus is the metaphysical mechanism of astral magic: the subtle pneumatic medium through which stellar influences travel from the heavens into the sublunary world and into prepared objects
Hermeticism is one of the primary strands of the prisca theologia; Hermes Trismegistus was placed at or near the origin of the ancient wisdom chain by Ficino and his successors
The ten sefirot of Kabbalah function as a parallel to Neoplatonic emanations; Christian Kabbalists explicitly mapped sephirotic structure onto Neoplatonic hypostases
